label: Hubert Laws Afro-Classic Ensemble/ Randy Weston Quintet

performers.name: Hubert Laws and his Afro-Classic Ensemble

quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
quotes.text: You talked about Eric Gale yesterday, bemoaning the fact that so many of the CTI artists are not with us. Another one of those killer albums, Eric Gale’s Forecast. I remember it well. You’re listed flute and piccolo on this one. Killing Me Softly, Dindi – which, that was big. I know when Wayne first put that on, I think, Super Nova. I think that might have been it. It might have been Super Nova or one of those ones. But anytime – again, Creed Taylor putting the rhythm section out there. He was going to make sure that everybody go their due. Carnegie Hall Concert, January 12th, 1973. You got – this is pretty interesting, because of the – I remember this cover. It’s got the multiple pictures of you all across.
quotes.caption: Hubert Laws recalls the Jan 12, 1973 Carnegie Hall concert
quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
quotes.text: I remember Billy Taylor was so excited about that record, because I remember we did a Carnegie Hall concert. I had Debra and Eloise and Ronnie. They all played on it. Had a big band, because CBS sponsored the – all these instruments. I had trumpets and trombones, because that’s what’s on that record. They supported strings. Back then, that’s when the record companies got behind supporting you with additional instruments and all that. I never will forget, boy, people in the aisle just rocking with that song. Billy Taylor came to the dressing room and said, “Man, that was a tremendous piece.” He complimented me on that. That meant a lot to me, because, you know, Billy Taylor’s a bebopper.
quotes.caption: Hubert Laws recalls 1973 Afro-Classic Ensemble concert with Billy Taylor
quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
